_metadata.description: Comprehensive database of harmful additives, contaminants, and concerning ingredients found in dietary supplements. Used for scoring penalties and consumer warnings.
_metadata.scoring_rule: Apply penalty points based on severity_level. High: -2.0, Moderate: -1.0, Low: -0.5. Multiple additives are cumulative. Scored in B1 section of score_supplements.py.

severity_level_definitions.high: Significant health concerns with strong evidence (e.g., IARC 2A/2B carcinogens, trans fats, heavy metal contaminants, endocrine disruptors)
severity_level_definitions.moderate: Potential health concerns, limit exposure (e.g., artificial sweeteners with WHO caution, synthetic colorants, emulsifiers with microbiome data)
severity_level_definitions.low: Minor concerns, generally safe at typical supplement doses (e.g., GRAS excipients, flow agents, natural thickeners)

fields.aliases: Alternative names for deterministic matching (E-numbers, CAS, IUPAC, brand names)
fields.cui: UMLS Concept Unique Identifier (top-level, lowercase) or null
fields.category: One of: colorant, colorant_artificial, colorant_natural, contaminant, emulsifier, excipient, fat_oil, filler, flavor, mineral_compound, nutrient_synthetic, phosphate, preservative, preservative_antioxidant, processing_aid, stimulant_laxative, sweetener, sweetener_artificial, sweetener_natural, sweetener_sugar_alcohol
fields.regulatory_status: US (FDA), EU (EFSA), WHO status with CFR/regulation citations and ADI values
fields.match_rules: Matching config: match_mode, fuzzy_threshold, case_sensitive, preferred_alias
fields.references_structured: Structured citations: type, authority, title, citation, url, published_date, evidence_grade, supports_claims
fields.external_ids: Optional: cas, pubchem_cid — present only when non-null
fields.jurisdictional_statuses: Per-jurisdiction status: approved, permitted_with_limit, restricted, warning_issued, banned, not_evaluated
fields.review: Governance: status, last_reviewed_at, reviewed_by, next_review_due, change_log
fields.dose_thresholds: ADI/TDI with value, unit, source — null if not established
fields.entity_relationships: Links to related entries or null

_metadata.pipeline_integration: Used by enrichment script to apply penalty points for harmful ingredients during scoring. Matches against ingredient names and other_ingredient forms.
sources: FDA Food Additive Status List / 21 CFR citations
_metadata.audit_notes: Full audit session 2026-03-22. Schema v5.1: removed top-level CUI→cui, removed label_tokens/regex/exposure_context/entity_type(ingredient)/class_tags, normalized external_ids casing, standardized references_structured fields, fixed jurisdictional status_codes. Removed critical severity tier (use banned_recalled for immediate hazards). Renamed 8 BANNED_ADD_ entries to ADD_. Fixed scoring_rule to match code. 18 verified audit fixes (P0-P2): Nickel TDI, Yellow 6 mechanism, Antimony IARC 2A, Advantame CFR, Blue 1 Southampton, Erythritol EFSA ADI, sulfites ADI withdrawal, methylparaben ratio, Ace-K ADI, Tin attribution, Neotame ref, MSG EFSA ADI, nitrites IARC qualifier, hydrogenated coconut oil name, Potassium Hydroxide/Sorbitol severity. 27 wrong aliases removed (MSG nucleotides, metal stearates, polysorbate cross-matching, glycerol softgel false positives, aspartame methyl ester). 16 placeholder mechanisms replaced with verified pathways. Chromium VI migrated to banned_recalled_ingredients.json. Categories normalized from 43 to 20. All changes verified against FDA/EFSA/IARC/JECFA/NTP primary sources. 2678/2678 tests passing. 2026-03-23 harmful_additives CUI hardening: added local audit runbook/CUI policy metadata, corrected live-verified wrong CUIs, split nitrites into parent+atomic children, converted synthetic antioxidants to disabled routing parent, and annotated intentional null-CUI umbrella/proprietary entries. 2026-03-23 GSRS/UNII hardening: added local verify_unii runbook/policy metadata, curated botanical alias coverage for canola oil, and governed-null GSRS handling for proprietary or non-exact registry matches so UNII runs stay deterministic.

audit_runbook.purpose: Keep harmful_additives.json structurally valid, CUI-audited, and safe for scoring without broad umbrella double-counting.
workflow: 1. Run verify_cui.py in dry-run mode first and inspect VERIFIED, INVALID_CUI, MISMATCH, MISSING_CUI, and NOT_FOUND buckets.
2. Auto-apply only exact missing-CUI matches with --apply; do not use --apply-mismatches unless a human has confirmed the replacement concept from live UMLS output.
3. Run verify_unii.py in dry-run mode to populate FDA/GSRS identity, CFR, active-moiety, and salt/parent context; auto-apply only when the GSRS match passes the local name/CAS gate.
4. Keep umbrella or policy records explicit: set cui to null, add cui_status/cui_note, and disable scoring only when a parent routes to atomic child entries.
5. Treat GSRS governed-null entries as intentional non-UNII records when the registry only exposes a broader mixture, proprietary system, or non-exact CAS variant.
6. After any edit, rerun the targeted regression suite and at least one live verify_cui.py and verify_unii.py pass before treating the file as production-clean.
7. Do not force a single CUI onto proprietary blends, formulation technologies, or grouped policy labels when UMLS does not expose one exact ingredient concept.

score_pipeline_notes.scorable_entries: Atomic ingredient records and routeable exact additives.
score_pipeline_notes.non_scorable_parents: Entries with entity_type=class or match_rules.match_mode=disabled are documentation/routing parents and must not double-count against their children.

release_gate_checklist: Review the verify_cui.py summary: VERIFIED entries should cover all atomic ingredients, while remaining ANNOTATED_NULL entries must already have approved cui_status/cui_note governance.

cui_audit_policy.goal: Every scorable atomic ingredient should have a correct CUI or an explicit, justified null annotation.
cui_audit_policy.exact_alias_policy: If live UMLS verification fails only because of a naming variant, add the verified synonym to aliases before accepting a null CUI.
cui_audit_policy.overwrite_policy: Existing non-null CUIs may be changed only when live UMLS verification shows the stored concept is wrong or obsolete.
cui_audit_policy.umbrella_policy: Grouped records should stay null-CUI unless broken into atomic children; when kept as parents they need member_ids or a clear routing explanation.
cui_audit_policy.proprietary_policy: Trade names, blends, and delivery systems may remain null only with a cui_note that explains why no single UMLS concept is authoritative.
cui_audit_policy.release_gate: Production acceptance requires zero invalid CUIs, zero unresolved mismatches, and zero unannotated null-CUI records for this file.

unii_audit_policy.goal: Populate exact GSRS/FDA identity where available without forcing a UNII onto mixtures, proprietary systems, or non-exact variant matches.
unii_audit_policy.authority_boundary: UNII, CFR sections, active moiety, salt/parent mappings, metabolic relationships, and opportunistic RxCUI come from GSRS. CAS and PubChem CID remain PubChem authority; CUI remains UMLS authority.
unii_audit_policy.exact_alias_policy: If GSRS has the right single substance under a botanical or regulatory synonym, add that verified synonym to aliases before accepting a null UNII.
unii_audit_policy.governed_null_policy: If GSRS only resolves to a broader mixture, proprietary system, or mismatched CAS variant, keep UNII null and let verify_unii.py classify the entry as governed_null instead of forcing a bad identity join.
unii_audit_policy.release_gate: Production acceptance requires zero unexpected GSRS rejections or not-found items for scorable atomic entries; only reviewed governed-null items may remain without a UNII.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: May alter gut microbiome composition and glucose metabolism at high doses. Methylene chloride (manufacturing byproduct) is present at trace levels well below safety thresholds.

regulatory_status.EU: E950 approved. EFSA 2025 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2025;23:e9317) raised ADI to 15 mg/kg/day (from previous 9 mg/kg/day set by SCF in 2000), now aligned with FDA and JECFA.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI of 0-15 mg/kg body weight

population_warnings: Pregnant women — limited long-term data on developmental effects
People with diabetes — some studies suggest effects on glucose metabolism
People with gut sensitivities — may alter microbiome composition at high doses
harmful_additives.notes: An artificial sweetener ~200× sweeter than sugar (E950). FDA-approved with an ADI of 15 mg/kg/day. Some studies suggest effects on gut microbiome composition and glucose/insulin response, but evidence is limited. WHO (2023) advises against long-term use of non-sugar sweeteners for weight control. Generally safe at typical supplement doses.

harmful_additives.notes: A processing contaminant formed when carbohydrate-rich ingredients are heated above 120°C. Classified as a probable human carcinogen by IARC (Group 2A). Not intentionally added to supplements, but may be present as a contaminant in processed botanical extracts. EFSA and FDA have set mitigation guidance.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolized to glycidamide via CYP2E1, which forms DNA adducts (N7-GA-Gua) and causes genotoxicity. Classified as IARC Group 2A (probable human carcinogen, Vol 60, 1994) based on sufficient evidence in animals and strong mechanistic evidence.

regulatory_status.US: Not banned but FDA has issued guidance to reduce acrylamide in food (2016). California Prop 65 listed.
regulatory_status.EU: Commission Regulation (EU) 2017/2158 sets benchmark levels for acrylamide in food.

population_warnings: Pregnant women — developmental neurotoxicity in animal studies
Children — higher exposure per kg body weight
General population — minimize exposure from processed ingredients

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic chemicals designed to mimic natural flavors. Exact composition is often proprietary and undisclosed on supplement labels. Some individual flavoring compounds have been linked to allergic reactions, headaches, and behavioral effects in sensitive individuals, though the category as a whole is FDA GRAS-approved.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved under GRAS (21 CFR 101.22). Manufacturers not required to disclose individual flavoring chemicals.
regulatory_status.EU: Approved under Regulation (EC) No 1334/2008 with mandatory labeling as 'flavouring'.

population_warnings: People with chemical sensitivities — undisclosed compounds may trigger reactions
Children — some synthetic flavoring agents linked to behavioral effects (Southampton study)
People with allergies — proprietary blends may contain undisclosed allergens
harmful_additives.notes: A broad category of synthetic flavoring agents used in supplement formulations. Individual chemicals are FDA GRAS-approved, but exact compositions are proprietary and not disclosed on labels. This lack of transparency makes it difficult to assess individual risk. Some flavoring compounds have been linked to allergic reactions and headaches. Presence signals a heavily processed formulation.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Metabolizes into aspartic acid, phenylalanine, and methanol. Phenylalanine is toxic to individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU) due to inability to metabolize it. Methanol is further metabolized to formaldehyde. IARC evaluated limited evidence of hepatocellular carcinoma in animal studies (Ramazzini Institute) and limited epidemiological evidence linking aspartame to hepatocellular carcinoma and non-Hodgkin lymphoma.

harmful_additives.notes: An intense artificial sweetener (~200× sweeter than sugar). Classified as IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic) in July 2023. Individuals with phenylketonuria (PKU) must avoid aspartame entirely. Metabolizes to phenylalanine, aspartic acid, and methanol. FDA and EFSA maintain established acceptable daily intake (ADI) levels. Commonly found in sugar-free supplement formulations.

harmful_additives.notes: A leavening and anti-caking agent that contains bioavailable aluminum. Contributes to total aluminum body burden. EFSA established a tolerable weekly intake (TWI) of 1 mg Al/kg body weight due to neurotoxicity concerns with chronic exposure. Used in supplement tablet manufacturing.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Releases bioavailable aluminum ions (Al³⁺) during digestion. Aluminum accumulates in bone and brain tissue with chronic exposure. EFSA identified developmental neurotoxicity as the critical endpoint for aluminum compounds, establishing a TWI of 1 mg/kg bw/week. Animal studies show aluminum-induced oxidative stress, mitochondrial dysfunction, and interference with calcium/iron homeostasis.

harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic chelating agent used as a preservative in supplements to bind metal ions and prevent oxidation. Can deplete essential minerals (zinc, iron, copper) when consumed regularly. FDA limits use to 75 ppm in food. Not recommended in mineral-containing supplement formulations where it may reduce bioavailability of the active ingredients. Cross-reference: shares E385 regulatory identity with ADD_DISODIUM_EDTA (disodium EDTA).
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Non-selective chelator that binds divalent and trivalent metal ions (Zn²⁺, Fe²⁺/Fe³⁺, Cu²⁺, Ca²⁺, Mn²⁺) with high affinity, forming stable complexes excreted renally. Chronic dietary exposure can deplete essential trace minerals. In supplements containing mineral ingredients, EDTA may chelate and reduce bioavailability of the intended active. Poorly absorbed orally (~5%), limiting systemic toxicity, but GI-level mineral binding still occurs.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved food additive under 21 CFR 172.120. Limited to specific foods; max 75–500 ppm depending on application.
regulatory_status.EU: E385 authorized with ADI of 2.5 mg/kg bw/day (SCF 1977, reconfirmed 1990). EFSA full re-evaluation pending (call for data issued 2024).

population_warnings: People taking mineral supplements — EDTA may chelate and reduce absorption of zinc, iron, copper, and calcium
People with mineral deficiencies — further depletion risk from chronic chelation
People with renal impairment — reduced clearance of EDTA-metal complexes

harmful_additives.notes: A highly refined vegetable oil used as a carrier or softgel fill in supplements. High-heat refining (deodorization at 200–270°C) may generate lipid oxidation products and trace trans fats. Predominantly omega-6 linoleic acid; chronic high intake may shift omega-6/omega-3 balance. Not banned anywhere. Quality concern rather than acute toxicity.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Refined canola oil undergoes high-heat processing (bleaching, deodorization at 200–270°C) that may generate lipid oxidation products (aldehydes, peroxides) and trace trans fatty acids. High omega-6 content (~20% linoleic acid) may contribute to pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways when dietary omega-6/omega-3 ratio is already elevated. Modern canola is bred for low erucic acid (<2%) to avoid cardiotoxicity concerns of traditional rapeseed. Primarily a quality/formulation signal rather than acute harm.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS (21 CFR 184.1555). Qualified health claim allowed for reduced saturated fat.
regulatory_status.EU: Allowed. Low erucic acid rapeseed oil authorized; erucic acid max 20 g/kg in vegetable oils (Reg 2019/1870 amending Reg 1881/2006).
regulatory_status.WHO: No specific ADI. WHO recommends balanced omega-6/omega-3 intake.

population_warnings: People with inflammatory conditions — high omega-6 intake may promote pro-inflammatory pathways
People concerned about oxidation products — refined oils contain aldehydes from high-heat processing

harmful_additives.notes: A cellulose-derived thickener and stabilizer used in supplement capsules and formulations. FDA GRAS status. Recent controlled-feeding studies suggest high chronic intake may affect gut microbiome, but typical supplement excipient doses are much lower than study levels. Not banned anywhere. Low-concern quality signal. SEVERITY UPGRADED TO MODERATE (2026): Human RCT data now exists. Randomized controlled-feeding study (Chassaing et al., Gastroenterology 2022, PMID 34774538): 15g/day CMC for 11 days in healthy adults caused significant gut microbiome perturbation, reduced microbial diversity, reduced short-chain fatty acids, and mucosal encroachment in 2 of 16 subjects. 2025 placebo-controlled trial (Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, 60 participants) confirmed microbiota composition changes and intestinal inflammation markers. No longer animal-only evidence — human mechanistic data materially elevates this from 'low'.

harmful_additives.notes: A red seaweed extract (E407/E407a) widely used as a thickener and emulsifier. FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.620) and EU-approved (E407). A 2024 BMC Medicine RCT (Wagner et al.) found carrageenan triggered gut inflammation and insulin resistance in overweight young men, and a 2025 study (Vissers et al.) showed amplified inflammatory markers in Crohn's disease epithelial cells. EFSA set a temporary ADI of 75 mg/kg bw/day in 2018. Classified as moderate because evidence is Tier 2 (emerging clinical research) rather than Tier 1 (regulatory action). People with IBD, IBS, or metabolic concerns should consider avoidance.

regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI: 2.5 mg/kg bw/day (established 1973, reaffirmed).

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Elevated circulating erythritol enhances platelet reactivity via increased intracellular calcium signaling, promoting thrombosis. Prospective cohorts link highest-quartile plasma erythritol to 1.8-2.2x increased MACE risk. At supplement doses (>20 g), osmotic laxation occurs via unabsorbed sugar alcohol drawing water into the intestinal lumen.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS via notification (GRN 789). No numerical ADI established by FDA.
regulatory_status.EU: E968 approved as sweetener. EFSA 2023 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2023;21(12):e8430) established ADI of 0.5 g/kg bw/day based on laxative threshold NOAEL. Panel noted exposure estimates exceed ADI for high consumers in all population groups.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA: ADI 'not specified' (1999), meaning no safety concern at normal dietary intake levels. Does not address high-dose supplemental use.

population_warnings: Cardiovascular disease or thrombotic risk — elevated MACE risk (HR 1.8-2.2) in prospective cohorts; enhanced platelet reactivity confirmed in RCT (PMID 39114916)
Post-MI or atrial fibrillation — avoid high-dose erythritol until cardiovascular signal is further characterized (PMID 39983608)
IBS or digestive sensitivity — osmotic laxation at >20 g/day (EFSA laxative NOAEL basis)
harmful_additives.notes: A sugar alcohol (C4 polyol) used as a zero-calorie sweetener in supplements and foods. Three independent human study lines support a cardiovascular/thrombotic risk signal: Nature Medicine 2023 prospective cohort (HR 1.80-2.21 for MACE), ATVB 2024 RCT confirming enhanced platelet reactivity in healthy volunteers, and JACC Advances 2025 ARIC cohort linking erythritol to heart failure and CHD. FDA GRAS (GRN 789) unchanged; EFSA set ADI at 0.5 g/kg bw/day in 2023 based on laxative threshold. Consumers with cardiovascular risk should limit high-dose erythritol intake.

Evidence is extrapolated from studies on related emulsifiers (CMC, polysorbate 80) which promoted low-grade inflammation and metabolic syndrome in mice. Direct human data on polyglycerol esters specifically is limited.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Mixture of sugar alcohols (sorbitol, maltitol, hydrogenated oligosaccharides) that are incompletely absorbed in the small intestine. population_warnings: IBS or digestive sensitivity — osmotic laxation at >20-30 g/day (individual tolerance varies)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Sugar alcohol (E965) partially absorbed in the small intestine (~40%). Unabsorbed portion fermented by colonic bacteria causing osmotic diarrhea. Higher glycemic index than most sugar alcohols (GI ~35-52 depending on form). Laxative effects documented at >10g single dose. JECFA ADI "not specified" (safe at typical use).

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Highly processed glucose polymer (DE 3-20) with glycemic index 85-105, comparable to pure glucose. Rapidly digested to glucose in the small intestine. In vitro studies suggest maltodextrin may suppress antimicrobial defense mechanisms and promote E. coli adhesion to intestinal epithelial cells (PMID 22394256). Used as cheap filler/binder in supplements with no nutritional benefit.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Petroleum-derived lubricant containing mineral oil saturated hydrocarbons (MOSH) and mineral oil aromatic hydrocarbons (MOAH). EFSA 2023 risk assessment found MOAH with 3+ aromatic rings are genotoxic and associated with DNA damage and carcinogenicity. MOSH can accumulate in human tissues. Oral mineral oil impairs absorption of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K), directly counterproductive in a supplement context. NOTE: Untreated/mildly treated mineral oils are IARC Group 1 carcinogen; pharmaceutical-grade white mineral oil used in supplements is IARC Group 3.

regulatory_status.US: FDA permitted under 21 CFR 172.878 with purity specifications limiting aromatic content.
regulatory_status.EU: Under active regulatory restriction. EU Commission draft regulation (late 2023) proposes maximum MOAH levels in food, expected adoption 2025 with applicability from 2027. France banned MOAH-containing mineral oils in food packaging inks as of Jan 2025.
regulatory_status.WHO: No specific position on supplement use; defers to national regulators.

population_warnings: People taking fat-soluble vitamin supplements (A, D, E, K) - May impair absorption
Children - Higher MOAH exposure concern per EFSA
Elderly - Risk of lipoid pneumonia from aspiration
harmful_additives.notes: IARC CLASSIFICATION CORRECTION: Untreated and mildly treated mineral oils are IARC Group 1 (KNOWN human carcinogen — occupational evidence for scrotal and skin cancers). Highly refined / pharmaceutical-grade white mineral oil = IARC Group 3 (not classifiable). EFSA 2023 assessment concluded MOAH fractions with 3+ aromatic rings pose a 'possible concern for human health.' While MOSH was found not to raise concerns at current levels, the safety margin is narrow. Mineral oil is an avoidable excipient -- better-formulated supplements use plant-based alternatives like magnesium stearate or vegetable-based release agents. EU is actively moving to restrict MOAH in food.
scientific_references: EFSA Journal 2023;21(9):8215 - Update of the risk assessment of mineral oil hydrocarbons in food
21 CFR 172.878 - White mineral oil (FDA)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Provides glutamate for umami taste receptors. Excitotoxicity concerns are theoretical at supplement doses; human pharmacokinetic data does not support significant elevation of systemic glutamate at normal dietary intake. Self-reported 'MSG sensitivity' is not consistently reproduced in double-blind placebo-controlled conditions.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS status but requires labeling
regulatory_status.EU: E621 approved. EFSA 2017 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2017;15(7):4910) established group ADI of 30 mg/kg bw/day (as glutamic acid) for E620-E625. EFSA noted exposure may exceed ADI for high consumers in all population groups.

population_warnings: People sensitive to MSG - May cause symptom complex

harmful_additives.notes: Umami flavor enhancer classified as FDA GRAS (21 CFR 182.1480). WHO/JECFA ADI: 'not specified' — safe at any realistic dietary intake. 'MSG symptom complex' (formerly 'Chinese restaurant syndrome'): multiple double-blind placebo-controlled trials have failed to consistently reproduce symptoms on rechallenge (PMID 9215242, PMID 11080723). This is largely a nocebo phenomenon in blinded conditions. Excitotoxicity at supplement doses is not supported by human pharmacokinetic data — dietary glutamate does not significantly raise plasma or brain glutamate at normal doses. Sodium content (12% by weight) may be relevant for hypertensive individuals at high food-level intake, not at typical supplement doses. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM MODERATE TO LOW based on controlled trial evidence.

harmful_additives.notes: An ultra-high-intensity artificial sweetener ~7,000–13,000× sweeter than sucrose. Chemically derived from aspartame but metabolized differently; phenylalanine release is negligible at typical use levels — no PKU warning required. REGULATORY NOTE: Previous JSON listed US ADI as 18 mg/kg/day — this is incorrect and has no basis in any regulatory publication. EFSA 2025 set ADI at 10 mg/kg/day (EFSA Journal 2025:9480), significantly higher than the earlier 2 mg/kg/day. 2024 in vitro data (PMID 38721028, Frontiers in Nutrition 2024): neotame caused intestinal epithelial apoptosis via T1R3 receptor signaling and promoted pathogenic transformation of E. coli and E. faecalis at physiologically relevant concentrations; human significance not yet established.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Aspartame derivative that is rapidly metabolized; phenylalanine exposure negligible at typical ultra-low usage levels. Potential gut epithelial concern: 2024 in vitro study (PMID 38721028) demonstrated neotame-induced intestinal epithelial cell apoptosis via T1R3 taste receptor signaling, disruption of tight junctions, and phenotypic shift of E. coli and E. faecalis toward pathogenicity at physiologically relevant concentrations. Human significance and in vivo replication pending.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved as general-purpose sweetener and flavor enhancer (21 CFR 172.829, 2002). ADI approximately 0.3 mg/kg bw/day based on JECFA evaluation used at approval. No PKU warning label required (unlike aspartame) at typical use levels.
regulatory_status.EU: E961; EFSA 2025 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2025:9480) established ADI of 10 mg/kg bw/day — a significant increase from the previous EFSA ADI of 2 mg/kg bw/day. Approved for use in foodstuffs and table-top sweeteners.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA 2003 evaluation: ADI 2 mg/kg bw/day. EFSA 2025 revision is substantially higher.

population_warnings: Gut sensitivity — 2024 in vitro data showed neotame-induced intestinal epithelial apoptosis and pathogenic bacterial transformation (PMID 38721028); human significance pending
PKU — unlike aspartame, phenylalanine release is negligible; no PKU warning required
scientific_references: PMID 38721028 — Neotame intestinal epithelial apoptosis and gut microbiome effects (Frontiers in Nutrition 2024)
EFSA Journal 2025:9480 — Re-evaluation of neotame (E961), ADI revised to 10 mg/kg bw/day

harmful_additives.notes: Heavy metal contaminant in supplements, primarily from botanical ingredients grown in nickel-rich soil. IARC Group 1 carcinogen (nickel compounds via inhalation — nasal/lung cancer); oral carcinogenicity less established. EFSA 2020 set TDI at 13 ug/kg bw/day. Primary supplement concern is allergic contact dermatitis in the ~15% of the population sensitized to nickel.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Oral nickel is poorly absorbed (1-5%). Absorbed nickel can trigger systemic contact dermatitis in sensitized individuals. Chronic oral exposure at elevated levels may cause gastrointestinal irritation. Nickel compounds are IARC Group 1 carcinogens via inhalation (nasal/lung cancer), but oral carcinogenicity is less established. EFSA 2020 identified benchmark dose for nickel as developmental toxicity endpoint.

regulatory_status.US: Not a permitted food additive or supplement ingredient. Treated as a contaminant. EPA oral RfD: 0.02 mg/kg/day (soluble nickel salts). IARC Group 1 carcinogen (nickel compounds, by inhalation — nasal and lung cancer). Oral carcinogenicity is less established. No FDA established supplement contaminant limit; monitored under general adulteration provisions.
regulatory_status.EU: EFSA 2020 established a TDI (tolerable daily intake) of 13 µg/kg bw per day for dietary nickel (EFSA Journal 2020;18(11):e06268). Previous TDI was 2.8 µg/kg bw/day. Mean dietary exposure can approach or exceed TDI in some population groups.
regulatory_status.WHO: IARC Group 1 (nickel compounds, inhalation). WHO Drinking Water Guideline: 0.07 mg/L. JECFA: no established ADI for food use — not a food additive.

population_warnings: Nickel-sensitized individuals — oral nickel can trigger systemic contact dermatitis flares at >0.3 mg single dose
Pregnant women — developmental toxicity endpoint identified by EFSA 2020
Children — higher exposure per body weight from diet

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Nitrite preservative that can form N-nitroso compounds (NOCs) in the acidic stomach environment, especially in the presence of secondary amines. NOCs are potent carcinogens (IARC Group 2A for ingested nitrite under conditions resulting in endogenous nitrosation). Also causes methemoglobinemia by oxidizing hemoglobin iron from Fe2+ to Fe3+, reducing oxygen-carrying capacity.

regulatory_status.US: FDA: regulated food preservative under 21 CFR 172.175. Strictly limited in cured meats. Not approved for supplement use.
regulatory_status.EU: E249 permitted in specific meat products with limits (max 150 mg/kg ingoing). EFSA 2017 re-evaluation.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI 0-0.07 mg/kg bw/day (as nitrite ion). IARC Group 2A (ingested nitrite under conditions of endogenous nitrosation).

population_warnings: Infants - elevated methemoglobinemia risk
Pregnant women - minimize exposure

Not expected in dietary supplements but tracked for completeness.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Nitrate preservative salt (KNO3). In the body, nitrate is reduced to nitrite by oral bacteria, then nitrite can form N-nitroso compounds (NOCs) in acidic stomach environment. NOCs are potent carcinogens. IARC Group 2A applies to ingested nitrate/nitrite under conditions of endogenous nitrosation. Distinct from nitrite: nitrate requires bacterial reduction first, so carcinogenic potential is indirect and dose-dependent.

regulatory_status.US: FDA: regulated food preservative (21 CFR 172.160). Permitted in cured meats with limits. Not intended for supplement use.
regulatory_status.EU: E252 permitted in specific meat products with limits. EFSA ADI 3.7 mg/kg bw/day as nitrate ion.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI 0-3.7 mg/kg bw/day (as nitrate ion). IARC Group 2A (ingested nitrate under conditions of endogenous nitrosation).

population_warnings: Infants — methemoglobinemia risk from nitrate-to-nitrite conversion; avoid unnecessary exposure

Refined palm oil can contain 3-MCPD and glycidyl ester contaminants formed during high-temperature processing. EFSA 2018 identified glycidyl esters as genotoxic and carcinogenic. In supplement context, amounts are small (capsule coatings, softgel fill) but flagged as quality signal.

harmful_additives.notes: Palm oil in supplements is typically present in small amounts (softgel fill, carrier oil). The primary concern is 3-MCPD/glycidyl ester contamination from refining, not saturated fat at supplement doses. Moderate severity as quality signal for processed oil use.

Mineral absorption interference claims are not supported at normal use levels.

harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic emulsifier that may be contaminated with 1,4-dioxane (a carcinogen). UPGRADED TO HIGH RISK based on 2024-2025 research showing severe gut microbiome disruption, chronic intestinal inflammation, and links to inflammatory bowel disease. 2024 study shows it accelerates age-related cognitive decline. 2025 research confirms it drives mucus-penetrating microbiota causing chronic inflammation. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM HIGH TO MODERATE (2026): Animal study doses demonstrating gut microbiome disruption (Chassaing et al., Nature 2015) were estimated at 100–500× human dietary exposure. ADDapt 2025 trial showed emulsifier-free diet associated with Crohn's remission, but trial used a MIXTURE of emulsifiers (P80 + CMC + carrageenan) in IBD patients — cannot isolate P80 effects. EMA review of polysorbate 80 as pharmaceutical excipient: 'no adverse reactions reported for oral tablet/capsule excipient use' in healthy populations. Human mechanistic data at supplement-level doses is lacking; high/high rating is not supported. Genuine concern warrants moderate/medium as precautionary stance.

harmful_additives.notes: Potassium hydroxide is an FDA GRAS processing aid (21 CFR 184.1631) used to adjust pH during supplement manufacturing. By the time the product reaches the consumer, KOH has been neutralized and only trace amounts remain. The 'caustic alkali' concern applies to the raw industrial chemical, not to finished supplement products. Penalized at moderate level because its presence indicates chemical processing, but it is not a direct health hazard at residual levels.

The caustic properties apply to the raw chemical, not to the neutralized trace amounts in finished products.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Contains hydroxyanthracene derivatives (HADs) including sennosides, emodin, and aloe-emodin. EFSA 2018 concluded HADs should be considered genotoxic and carcinogenic unless proven otherwise. EFSA was unable to establish any safe daily intake level. Acts as a stimulant laxative causing chronic dependency, electrolyte imbalance (hypokalemia leading to cardiac arrhythmias), melanosis coli, and documented liver injury including acute liver failure.

regulatory_status.US: Regulated as an OTC drug (stimulant laxative) in the US, not as a dietary supplement ingredient. FDA's 2002 final rule (Fed. Reg. 67:31125, May 9 2002) removed ALOE and CASCARA SAGRADA from the OTC stimulant laxative monograph as not GRASE — senna/sennosides were NOT part of that ruling. Senna sennosides are separately subject to ongoing FDA OTC monograph review. DSHEA allows sale as a supplement ingredient, but drug-level stimulant laxative effects mean it is inappropriate as a routine supplement additive. FDA has not established a GRAS status for senna sennosides as a food/supplement ingredient.
regulatory_status.EU: EFSA 2018 and 2024: HADs are genotoxic and carcinogenic concern. EU attempted ban under Regulation 2021/468, annulled on procedural grounds Nov 2024, but scientific concerns unchanged.
regulatory_status.Canada: Health Canada permits for max 7 days with mandatory warnings.
regulatory_status.WHO: WHO Monographs acknowledge short-term traditional use only.

Chronic use causes dependency, electrolyte imbalance, melanosis coli, and documented liver injury. NIH LiverTox documents clinically apparent liver injury from senna abuse.

harmful_additives.notes: FDA GRAS Affirmed (21 CFR Part 184). Benzene formation with vitamin C is documented concern. Moved from banned_recalled to harmful_additives (2026-02-18). Low severity reflects benzene risk when combined with ascorbic acid. SEVERITY UPGRADED FROM LOW TO MODERATE (2026): Benzene formation with ascorbic acid is not merely theoretical in the supplement context — liquid supplement formulations routinely contain both sodium benzoate and ascorbic acid (vitamin C). Benzene is an IARC Group 1 carcinogen with no established safe threshold. FDA 2006 survey of beverages found some products exceeded 5 ppb benzene from this reaction. Hyperactivity association supported beyond Southampton pediatric study: PMID 22538314 found sodium benzoate-rich beverage intake significantly associated with ADHD symptom reporting in college students. The combination in a supplement product (sodium benzoate + vitamin C) represents a realistic benzene-formation risk that warrants moderate rather than low severity.

harmful_additives.notes: SAFETY CONCERN: Semi-synthetic derivative of chlorophyll with copper substituted for magnesium. Limited long-term safety data for regular consumption. Potential copper accumulation concerns with chronic use. While over 50 years of use and FDA GRAS status, recent studies question safety of regular copper chlorophyllin consumption. May cause gastrointestinal upset in sensitive individuals. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM MODERATE TO LOW (2026): EFSA 2015 re-evaluation (EFSA Journal 2015:4151) confirmed safety at authorized use levels. FDA permanently listed sodium copper chlorophyllin as a color additive in 2002 (21 CFR 73.125) with ADI calculated at 450 mg/person/day (with 200-fold safety factor). Acute toxicity: no toxicity observed up to 5000 mg/kg bw in animals. Copper content at typical colorant use levels is far below concerns for copper toxicity. Note: category corrected from 'colorant_artificial' to 'colorant_semisynthetic' — this pigment is derived from natural chlorophyll (saponification + copper replacement), not fully synthetic, and is exempt from FDA color certification.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Semi-synthetic derivative of chlorophyll with copper replacing magnesium. At typical colorant doses, copper content is far below toxicity thresholds. EFSA 2015 confirmed safety at authorized use levels. Theoretical concern only at extreme intake via copper accumulation in individuals with Wilson's disease.

regulatory_status.US: FDA exempt from certification (21 CFR 73.125). Permitted in citrus-based dry beverage mixes and certain other food categories at specified levels. ADI: 15 mg/kg bw/day (JECFA 2004).
regulatory_status.EU: E141(ii) — permitted food colorant (copper complexes of chlorophyllins) under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. ADI: 15 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA 2015 re-evaluation — EFSA Journal 13(5):4081). Restricted to specific food categories (spirits, vegetable preparations, certain cheeses).
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI: 15 mg/kg bw/day (copper complexes of chlorophylls and chlorophyllins, 2004). No significant toxicity concerns at ADI levels.

population_warnings: General population - review specific concerns
Wilson's disease / hepatic copper accumulation disorders: theoretical concern at high intake of copper-containing colorants
scientific_references: EFSA Journal 2015:4151 — Re-evaluation of chlorophylls (E140) and chlorophyllins (E141): safety confirmed at authorized levels
FDA Federal Register 2002:02-12544 — Permanent listing of sodium copper chlorophyllin (21 CFR 73.125)

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Polyphosphate salt contributing significant inorganic phosphorus absorbed at 90-100% efficiency (vs 40-60% for natural food phosphorus). EFSA 2019 re-evaluation established group ADI of 40 mg phosphorus/kg bw/day, which is already exceeded in infants, toddlers, and children at mean intake levels. Elevated serum phosphorus is associated with increased cardiovascular disease, vascular calcification, and accelerated kidney disease progression even within the 'normal' lab range. Approximately 10% of the general population has some degree of CKD with reduced renal function.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS as sequestrant when used per good manufacturing practice (21 CFR 182).
regulatory_status.EU: E452i approved. EFSA 2019 re-evaluation set group ADI of 40 mg P/kg bw/day for all phosphate additives (E 338-452). ADI already exceeded in children.
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA established maximum tolerable daily intake for phosphorus.

population_warnings: CKD/kidney disease — impaired phosphorus excretion; hyperphosphatemia accelerates renal decline
Cardiovascular disease — excess phosphate linked to vascular calcification
Children — EFSA noted group phosphate ADI already exceeded in children
Osteoporosis risk — chronic excess phosphorus disrupts Ca:P ratio
harmful_additives.notes: While SHMP has low acute toxicity, the inorganic phosphorus it contributes is a legitimate public health concern. EFSA's 2019 re-evaluation was the first time a numerical ADI was set for phosphates (previously 'not specified'), reflecting growing evidence of harm. A supplement intended to promote health should not add unnecessary inorganic phosphorus, especially given that the ADI is already exceeded in vulnerable populations.

harmful_additives.notes: A synthetic emulsifier and surfactant that can cause skin and gastrointestinal irritation. May disrupt the gut microbiome and cause digestive issues in sensitive individuals. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM HIGH TO MODERATE (2026): EMA background review on sodium laurilsulfate as pharmaceutical excipient (EMA/CHMP/351898/2014) explicitly states: 'no adverse reactions reported when used as an excipient in tablets and capsules.' Aphthous ulcer association is the best-established clinical harm — applies to oral care products (toothpaste) with direct/prolonged mucosal contact, not to tablets/capsules. Gut permeability concerns are supported by animal/ex vivo studies but lack human confirmation at supplement excipient doses. Endocrine disruption applies to concentrated topical/occupational exposure, not to oral supplement excipient quantities (typically <2% of formulation). Moderate is the appropriate designation for oral excipient use.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Anionic surfactant that disrupts lipid bilayer integrity through electrostatic interaction with cell membrane proteins. At concentrated topical exposure: causes protein denaturation, aphthous ulcers (oral mucosa), and skin irritation. At oral tablet/capsule excipient doses (<2% of formulation): EMA review found no adverse reactions. Gut permeability effects demonstrated in animal/ex vivo models but not confirmed at supplement excipient quantities in humans.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved food additive (21 CFR 172.822) as an emulsifying agent. Also FDA-recognized OTC pharmaceutical inactive ingredient. At concentrations in food/supplements (typically <1%), FDA considers safe. Higher concentrations (skin/mucosa) linked to irritation — relevant to oral dissolution products.
regulatory_status.EU: E487 — permitted emulsifier/stabilizer under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Quantum satis in specific food categories. EFSA 2018 re-evaluation: no safety concern at estimated dietary exposure (EFSA Journal 16(2):5080). Some EU member states restrict use in cosmetics at higher concentrations.
regulatory_status.WHO: No established ADI — considered safe at typical food use levels. No carcinogenicity classification. GI and mucosal irritant at high concentrations.

population_warnings: Oral mucosa sensitivity — SLS disrupts lipid bilayer; linked to aphthous ulcer recurrence in susceptible individuals
IBS — potential GI mucosal irritant at higher concentrations
scientific_references: EMA/CHMP/351898/2014 — Background review on sodium laurilsulfate: no adverse reactions in oral tablet/capsule excipient use
PMC4651417 — Human and environmental toxicity of SLS review

harmful_additives.notes: Powerful preservative that releases sulfur dioxide. Major allergen and asthma trigger. Required to be labeled. Can cause severe reactions including anaphylaxis in sensitive individuals.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Releases sulfur dioxide (SO₂) in aqueous solution. SO₂ triggers bronchoconstriction in sulfite-sensitive asthmatics via cholinergic reflex and possibly IgE-mediated mechanisms. Also acts as a reducing agent that can deplete vitamin B1 (thiamine). EFSA 2016 group ADI 0.7 mg SO₂ eq/kg bw/day (subsequently withdrawn 2022 — Margin of Exposure approach adopted).

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS (21 CFR 182.3766). Preservative and antioxidant. BANNED in fresh fruits and vegetables intended for retail sale (21 CFR 101.100(a)(4), since 1986). Must be declared on label when used as a preservative. Allergen — can trigger severe asthma and anaphylaxis in sulfite-sensitive individuals (~1% population, ~5% of asthmatics).
regulatory_status.EU: E223 — permitted preservative/antioxidant under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Sulfites group temporary ADI of 0.7 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA 2016) was WITHDRAWN in 2022 (EFSA Journal 2022;20(11):7594) due to insufficient toxicity data. EFSA adopted a Margin of Exposure (MOE) approach; MOEs below 80 indicate safety concern. Mandatory allergen declaration when ≥10 mg/kg or ≥10 mg/L as SO₂ equivalents (EU Food Information Regulation No 1169/2011, Annex II).
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA group ADI for sulfites (as SO₂): 0–0.7 mg/kg bw/day. Well-documented allergen/bronchoconstrictor in sensitive populations.

population_warnings: Asthma — SO2 triggers bronchoconstriction in ~5% of asthmatics; can be severe/life-threatening
Sulfite sensitivity — affects ~1% of general population; anaphylaxis documented
Children — lower body weight means lower absolute sulfite tolerance

harmful_additives.notes: Synthetic emulsifier. Generally considered safe but may affect gut barrier function and microbiome in high doses. Commonly used in softgels. Sorbitan esters are structurally related to but distinct from polysorbates — limited evidence that sorbitan monostearate shares polysorbate gut microbiome effects.

harmful_additives.notes: Preservative gas used in dried fruits and some supplements. Potent respiratory irritant. Can trigger asthma attacks and allergic reactions. Must be declared on labels.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Direct respiratory irritant and bronchoconstrictor. Mechanism: SO₂ stimulates airway sensory nerve endings (C-fibers) triggering parasympathetic bronchoconstriction reflex. Also generates sulfite/bisulfite ions that can damage proteins and DNA through oxidative mechanisms. Destroys thiamine (vitamin B1) in food.

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS (21 CFR 182.3862). Preservative and antioxidant. BANNED in fresh fruits and vegetables intended for retail sale (21 CFR 170.60/101.100(a)(4), 1986). Mandatory allergen label declaration when ≥10 ppm (21 CFR 101.100(a)(4)). Can trigger severe bronchoconstriction and anaphylaxis in sulfite-sensitive individuals.
regulatory_status.EU: E220 — permitted preservative/antioxidant under Regulation (EC) No 1333/2008. Sulfites group temporary ADI of 0.7 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA 2016) was WITHDRAWN in 2022 (EFSA Journal 2022;20(11):7594). EFSA adopted MOE approach; MOEs below 80 indicate safety concern. Mandatory allergen labeling when ≥10 mg/kg or ≥10 mg/L as SO₂ equivalents (EU Food Information Regulation No 1169/2011, Annex II).
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA group ADI for sulfites (as SO₂): 0–0.7 mg/kg bw/day. Bronchoconstrictor — significant asthma trigger. WHO Food Additives Series 30 (1993).

population_warnings: Asthma — SO2 is a potent bronchoconstrictor in ~5% of asthmatics; can be life-threatening
Sulfite sensitivity — affects ~1% of general population; anaphylaxis documented
Children — lower body weight means lower absolute sulfite tolerance

harmful_additives.notes: MODERATE RISK: When 'Tapioca' appears as ingredient (not 'tapioca fiber'), it's typically refined starch used as cheap filler. Has high glycemic index and provides no nutritional benefit. Indicates low-quality formulation. Tapioca FIBER is different and acceptable. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM MODERATE TO LOW (2026): Tapioca starch has no significant toxicological concerns. As a supplement excipient (capsule/tablet), amounts are trace and glycemic impact is clinically negligible. High GI concern is relevant only in gummy supplements where tapioca starch is a primary ingredient constituting a meaningful percentage of carbohydrate load — not for encapsulation excipient use. harmful_additives.notes: A heavy metal that can contaminate supplements, particularly those sourced from regions with industrial pollution. Can cause gastrointestinal issues and potentially affect nervous system function.
harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Inorganic tin (Sn²⁺/Sn⁴⁺) causes acute GI irritation (nausea, vomiting, diarrhea) at >200 mg/kg food. Poorly absorbed orally. Organotin compounds (tributyltin, etc.) are highly neurotoxic and immunotoxic but not relevant to dietary supplement context. JECFA PTWI 14 mg/kg bw (maintained since 1988).

regulatory_status.US: Not a recognized dietary supplement ingredient. Treated as a contaminant. Codex Alimentarius maximum levels for inorganic tin: 250 mg/kg (solid foods) and 150 mg/kg (beverages) in canned products.
regulatory_status.EU: Commission Regulation (EC) No 1881/2006 (as amended by Reg EU 2023/915) sets maximum levels for inorganic tin: 200 mg/kg (canned food) and 100 mg/kg (canned beverages).
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA established PTWI of 14 mg/kg bw for inorganic tin (1988, reaffirmed 2001/2006). Acute gastric irritation observed at ≥150 mg/kg in beverages and ≥250 mg/kg in solid foods.

population_warnings: GI sensitivity — acute gastric irritation at ≥150 mg/kg in beverages
Children — lower body weight means lower absolute tin tolerance
Consumers of canned supplements — primary tin exposure route from can lining migration

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Azo dye metabolized to aromatic amines in the GI tract. Associated with hyperactivity in children (McCann et al. 2007 Southampton study, Lancet). Cross-reactivity with aspirin in aspirin-sensitive individuals (urticaria, bronchospasm). Trace benzidine (IARC Group 1 carcinogen) present as manufacturing impurity in azo dyes; modern manufacturing limits this substantially. Some in vitro genotoxicity signals reported; EFSA 2009 re-evaluation found no confirmed in vivo genotoxicity at authorized food use levels. EFSA requested further Comet assay studies for sulfonated mono-azo dyes as a class.

regulatory_status.US: FDA approved as FD&C Yellow No. 5 (Tartrazine) — 21 CFR 74.705. Requires specific labeling on all products containing it (FALCPA). NOTE: E102 is the EU code — in the US this dye is designated FD&C Yellow No. 5, not E102. HHS/FDA April 2025 announced voluntary phase-out of all petroleum-based synthetic dyes by 2026.
regulatory_status.EU: Authorized as E102. ADI: 7.5 mg/kg bw/day (EFSA 2009 reduced from 10 mg/kg bw/day). EU Regulation 1333/2008 Annex V requires hyperactivity warning label on foods containing E102: 'may have an adverse effect on activity and attention in children.'
regulatory_status.WHO: JECFA ADI: 7.5 mg/kg bw/day.

Cross-reactivity with aspirin documented. EFSA 2009 re-evaluation reduced ADI from 10 to 7.5 mg/kg bw/day. FDA/HHS announced voluntary phase-out of petroleum-based synthetic dyes (April 2025, target end of 2026). harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic phenolic antioxidant. IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic) based on forestomach tumors in rodents. The rat forestomach mechanism has limited human relevance (humans lack a forestomach), but IARC maintained the 2B classification as a conservative measure. NTP 15th Report on Carcinogens: 'reasonably anticipated to be a human carcinogen.' Potential endocrine disruption (thyroid effects in animal studies; not confirmed at food-additive levels). FDA post-market reassessment launched February 2026 — regulatory outcome pending.
harmful_additives.notes: Petroleum-derived antioxidant preservative. IARC Group 2B (possibly carcinogenic); NTP lists as 'reasonably anticipated human carcinogen'. SEVERITY CORRECTED FROM CRITICAL TO HIGH: 'Critical' is reserved for IARC Group 1 / confirmed human carcinogens. BHA is Group 2B — limited animal evidence, limited human evidence — justifying 'high' not 'critical'. EU E320 is APPROVED (not banned) — previous entry implied it was restricted/banned. FDA February 2026 formal safety reassessment is a significant active regulatory development. California Prop 65 listed since 1990.

regulatory_status.US: PARTIALLY hydrogenated coconut oil (PHO): FDA banned all PHOs as GRAS effective June 18, 2018. Federal Register 2023-16725 (direct final rule, effective December 22, 2023) revoked remaining PHO uses — FDA PHO ban is complete as of December 2023. FULLY hydrogenated coconut oil: not subject to the PHO ban; contains no trans fats. Different regulatory status depending on degree of hydrogenation.
regulatory_status.EU: Partially hydrogenated fats subject to maximum trans fat limits (Regulation (EU) 2019/649, effective April 2021: max 2g per 100g fat in food). Fully hydrogenated fats are permitted.
regulatory_status.WHO: WHO recommends eliminating industrially produced trans fats (REPLACE action package, 2018).

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: PARTIAL hydrogenation creates artificial trans fatty acids (elaidic acid, etc.). Trans fats raise LDL cholesterol, lower HDL cholesterol, promote inflammation, and are independently associated with cardiovascular disease risk. FULL hydrogenation converts all double bonds to saturated bonds — primarily stearic and lauric acids. Fully hydrogenated coconut oil contains no trans fats; its profile is dominated by saturated fats (stearic acid metabolized to oleic acid; lauric acid raises HDL as well as LDL). This entry covers PARTIALLY hydrogenated coconut oil — the form banned by FDA.
harmful_additives.notes: IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: Partially hydrogenated vs. fully hydrogenated coconut oil have fundamentally different safety profiles and regulatory statuses. PARTIALLY hydrogenated coconut oil: source of artificial trans fats; FDA PHO ban complete December 2023 (Federal Register 2023-16725). High severity. FULLY hydrogenated coconut oil: no trans fats; saturated fat profile; not banned; lower concern. This entry covers the partially hydrogenated form. Severity raised from low to high to reflect the trans fat burden and FDA ban status. Confidence assigned as high.
scientific_references: FDA Final Determination 2015 — Partially Hydrogenated Oils (PHOs) no longer GRAS

PMC4016047 — Trans fatty acids and cardiovascular disease: meta-analysis
population_warnings: Cardiovascular disease — partially hydrogenated form contains trans fats (FDA banned PHOs 2018/2023)
Note: FULLY hydrogenated coconut oil contains NO trans fats and is NOT subject to PHO ban

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Weak estrogenic activity (approximately 2,500,000× less potent than 17β-estradiol (methylparaben is the weakest paraben; the ~100,000× figure from Routledge 1998 applies to butylparaben, not methylparaben — Miller et al. 2001 Env Health Perspect found methylparaben potency <1/3,000,000 relative to estradiol) at ERα in vitro). Darbre et al. studies detected parabens in breast tumor tissue, but this demonstrates exposure, not causation — parabens are also present in non-tumor breast tissue; no dose-response established. Scientific consensus does not support a causal link between methylparaben and breast cancer. Antiandrogenic effects weaker than propylparaben or butylparaben. France 2025 CLH proposal for environmental endocrine disrupter classification is an emerging regulatory signal.
harmful_additives.notes: Preservative with the weakest endocrine activity among common parabens. EU E218 remains APPROVED in food and cosmetics — previous entry description implied it was restricted like propylparaben/butylparaben. Severity downgraded from high to moderate: estrogenic potency is ~100,000× weaker than estradiol; EFSA/SCCS regulatory assessments confirm safety at current use levels. The Denmark/EU children's product restrictions primarily applied to butylparaben and propylparaben, not methylparaben. Added confidence=medium. France June 2025 CLH environmental endocrine disrupter proposal is a developing signal to monitor.

regulatory_status.EU: E216 BANNED as a food additive in the EU since 2006 — EFSA 2004 opinion found the previous ADI no longer valid due to male reproductive effects (reduced testosterone, reduced sperm count) in juvenile male rats (Oishi 2002). This is the most significant regulatory fact about propylparaben and was MISSING from the previous entry. EU cosmetics: restricted to 0.14% maximum in leave-on products; prohibited in cosmetics for children under 3 years; additional national restrictions apply. UK: also prohibits propylparaben as food additive (E216).
regulatory_status.WHO: Endocrine disruption concern based on EFSA 2004 assessment. EMA PDI: 2 mg/kg bw/day for oral pharmaceutical use.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Estrogenic activity stronger than methylparaben (though still weaker than estradiol). Antiandrogenic effects: reduces testosterone biosynthesis and serum testosterone in juvenile male rats (Oishi 2002 — the study that drove EU food ban). Reduces sperm count and motility in animal studies. EU food ban (2006) was explicitly based on male reproductive toxicity at doses near acceptable use levels. Systemic bioavailability after oral dosing is higher than methylparaben. At typical supplemental doses in adults, direct hormonal impact is uncertain but regulatory agencies have adopted a precautionary approach.
harmful_additives.notes: Preservative with the strongest documented endocrine disruption evidence among permitted parabens. CRITICAL REGULATORY FACT PREVIOUSLY MISSING: Propylparaben (E216) has been BANNED as a food additive in the EU since 2006 and in the UK — based on EFSA 2004 finding of male reproductive toxicity in juvenile rats. California Food Safety Act bans propylparaben in food from January 2027. SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM CRITICAL TO HIGH: Evidence is substantial (EU food ban, animal reproductive toxicity, antiandrogenic effects) but based on animal studies at doses above typical human supplemental exposure; 'critical' implies confirmed major human harm at realistic exposures, which is not yet established. 'High' accurately reflects the regulatory concern and mechanistic evidence.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Synthetic antioxidant preservative. NOT classified by IARC (not in Group 2B, 2A, or 1 — formally unclassified, distinct from Group 3). EFSA 2004: no carcinogenicity concern identified. NTP: not listed as carcinogen. EMERGING IMMUNOTOXICITY CONCERN: EWG-commissioned ToxCast analysis and peer-reviewed animal studies (PMC9147452, Frontiers in Immunology 2022) demonstrate that TBHQ: modulates T-cell function (Th2 skewing), may suppress vaccine efficacy, and exacerbates food allergy in animal models. FDA has not reviewed TBHQ for immunotoxicity since 1972 — a significant regulatory data gap. Japan prohibits TBHQ; EU permits with limits; US FDA approves with 0.02% fat content ceiling.
harmful_additives.notes: Petroleum-derived antioxidant preservative. Japan prohibits; FDA and EU permit with limits. IARC STATUS CLARIFIED: TBHQ is NOT classified by IARC (not Group 2B and not Group 3 — formally unclassified). SEVERITY DOWNGRADED FROM CRITICAL TO HIGH: 'Critical' requires confirmed major human harm. TBHQ has no carcinogen classification from IARC, NTP, or EFSA; however, the emerging immunotoxicity signal (PMC9147452), Japan prohibition, and 50-year FDA safety data gap justify 'high' as precautionary. FDA ADI note: 0.02% of fat content is a use limit, not an ADI per se; JECFA international ADI = 0.7 mg/kg bw/day.

harmful_additives.mechanism_of_harm: Sugar alcohol with dose-dependent osmotic laxative effect; ingestion above 20-30 g/day commonly causes bloating, gas, and diarrhea. A 2024 Cleveland Clinic study (PMID 38814334) found elevated circulating xylitol levels associated with increased platelet reactivity and major adverse cardiovascular events in a prospective cohort. Highly toxic to dogs at any dose (hepatic necrosis).

regulatory_status.US: FDA GRAS (21 CFR 172.395). Approved food additive. No established ADI; FDA advises labeling for laxative effect above 50 g/day.
regulatory_status.EU: E967 approved. EFSA 2023 sweeteners re-evaluation programme ongoing. Current ADI: not specified (acceptable at current use levels).

population_warnings: People with IBS or sensitive digestion — osmotic diarrhea at >20-30 g/day
Children — more susceptible to GI effects from sugar alcohols at lower doses
People with cardiovascular risk factors — emerging 2024 data links elevated xylitol to platelet reactivity and MACE (PMID 38814334); caution advised pending confirmation
Dog owners — xylitol is acutely hepatotoxic to dogs; cross-contamination risk from supplement handling
harmful_additives.notes: A 5-carbon sugar alcohol widely used as a sweetener in supplements, gummies, and chewables. Well-established anticariogenic properties (dental health). GI side effects are dose-dependent and generally mild. A 2024 Cleveland Clinic study raised cardiovascular safety questions but regulatory agencies have not yet changed the GRAS determination. Severity set to moderate based on GI effects at supplement-relevant doses plus the emerging CV signal.
